content.text: Managing Resource Lifecycle

Deployment Stacks track a set of managed resources — every resource declared in the current Bicep template. When you update the stack (az stack sub set), three things happen: resources in the new template that already exist are updated in-place (idempotent ARM PUT), new resources in the template are created and added to the managed set, and resources no longer in the template are either detached (left in Azure but removed from stack tracking) or deleted, depending on the --action-on-unmanage setting. This is fundamentally different from ARM deployments in Incremental mode, which never remove resources.

attrs.alt: Stack resource lifecycle: update behavior adding/removing resources, detachAll vs deleteAll, and stack delete options

content.text: actionOnUnmanage controls what happens to resources removed from the template during az stack sub set OR when the entire stack is deleted. For landing zone stacks managing production workloads, always use detachAll — deleting a policy assignment or resource group unexpectedly from a deleteAll stack causes immediate operational impact.
actionOnUnmanage Reference
Setting
Resource removed from template
Stack deleted
Use Case
detachAll
Resource stays in Azure, removed from stack tracking
All resources stay in Azure; stack object deleted
Production — resources outlive governance changes; policy assignments persist after being removed from stack
deleteAll
Resource deleted from Azure
All resources deleted from Azure
Dev/sandbox — full teardown when stack is deleted; resources tied to stack lifecycle
deleteResources (partial)
Resource deleted; resource groups kept
Resource groups kept; contained resources deleted
Rarely used — asymmetric cleanup for specific scenarios
Creating Stacks with Lifecycle Settings

Specify --action-on-unmanage at stack creation. The setting can be changed at any az stack sub set call — changing from detachAll to deleteAll on the next update means the next resource removal from the template will be deleted, not detached. Validate the setting before applying updates that remove resources from the template. Use az stack sub validate (what-if) to preview which resources would be affected before committing.

# Create a Deployment Stack with appropriate actionOnUnmanage

TARGET_SUB='<landing-zone-sub-id>'

# Production: detachAll — removed resources stay in Azure but lose stack management
# Use this for landing zones where workload resources should survive governance changes
az stack sub create \
  --name 'landing-zone-baseline' \
  --location eastus \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --template-file landing_zone_baseline.bicep \
  --parameters @landing_zone_baseline.prod.parameters.json \
  --deny-settings-mode DenyDelete \
  --deny-settings-apply-to-child-scopes \
  --action-on-unmanage detachAll

# Dev/sandbox: deleteAll — removing resources from template deletes them from Azure
# Use for disposable environments where auto-cleanup is desirable
az stack sub create \
  --name 'dev-environment' \
  --location eastus \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --template-file dev_environment.bicep \
  --parameters @dev.parameters.json \
  --deny-settings-mode None \
  --action-on-unmanage deleteAll

# Show current stack with managed resource count
az stack sub show \
  --name 'landing-zone-baseline' \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --query '{
    Status: provisioningState,
    DenyMode: denySettings.mode,
    ActionOnUnmanage: actionOnUnmanage.resources,
    ManagedResources: length(resources)
  }' -o json

# List all managed resources
az stack sub show \
  --name 'landing-zone-baseline' \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --query 'resources[].{Id:id, Status:status}' -o table
Updating Stacks and Resource Set Changes

Each az stack sub set call reconciles the current Azure state against the new template. Added resources are created. Changed resources are updated. Removed resources follow actionOnUnmanage. The update is idempotent for unchanged resources — an update that only adds tags to one resource will not modify any others. After an update, verify new resources appear in the managed set. To re-import a previously detached resource, simply add it back to the Bicep template — the next stack set call will detect the existing resource and add it to the managed set.

# Update a stack — add, update, and remove managed resources

TARGET_SUB='<landing-zone-sub-id>'

# Validate the update (what-if) before applying
az stack sub validate \
  --name 'landing-zone-baseline' \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --template-file landing_zone_baseline_v2.bicep \
  --parameters @landing_zone_baseline.prod.parameters.json \
  --deny-settings-mode DenyDelete \
  --action-on-unmanage detachAll

# Apply the update
# New resources in the template: created and added to managed set
# Changed resources: updated in-place (ARM PUT idempotent)
# Removed resources: detached (detachAll) or deleted (deleteAll)
az stack sub set \
  --name 'landing-zone-baseline' \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --template-file landing_zone_baseline_v2.bicep \
  --parameters @landing_zone_baseline.prod.parameters.json \
  --deny-settings-mode DenyDelete \
  --deny-settings-apply-to-child-scopes \
  --action-on-unmanage detachAll

# After update, verify new resource is managed
az stack sub show \
  --name 'landing-zone-baseline' \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --query 'resources[?contains(id, `applicationInsights`)].id' -o tsv

# Re-import a detached resource back into the stack
# If a resource was previously detached (removed from template then re-added),
# az stack sub set will re-import it on the next update — no manual action needed.
# The stack detects the existing resource and adds it back to the managed set.

# Check detached resources: Resources are no longer in stack but exist in Azure
# There is no direct CLI query for 'previously detached from stack X' resources.
# Compare what the stack manages vs what exists in the subscription:
MANAGED=$(az stack sub show \
  --name 'landing-zone-baseline' \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --query 'resources[].id' -o tsv | sort)

ALL_IN_RG=$(az resource list \
  --resource-group 'rg-workloads-prod-eastus' \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --query '[].id' -o tsv | sort)

# Resources in RG but NOT in stack = detached or never managed
comm -23 <(echo "$ALL_IN_RG") <(echo "$MANAGED")
Deleting Stacks

az stack sub delete removes the stack object and releases deny settings. Without --delete-all, managed resources stay in Azure but become unmanaged. With --delete-all, all managed resources are permanently deleted — resource groups, policy assignments, role assignments, VMs, storage accounts — everything the stack was tracking. This is the correct pattern for ephemeral dev environments but extremely dangerous for production stacks. The --delete-resource-groups flag is a middle ground: resource groups are deleted (cascading to everything inside them) but subscription-level resources like policy and role assignments are kept.

# Delete a Deployment Stack

TARGET_SUB='<landing-zone-sub-id>'

# Option A: Delete stack only, keep all resources (detach behavior)
# Managed resources remain in Azure but are no longer stack-managed.
# Deny settings removed; resources can be freely modified/deleted again.
az stack sub delete \
  --name 'landing-zone-baseline' \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --yes
# Note: --yes skips interactive confirmation

# Option B: Delete stack AND all managed resources (complete teardown)
# WARNING: Deletes ALL tracked resources — resource groups, VMs, storage,
# policy assignments, role assignments, etc. This is permanent.
az stack sub delete \
  --name 'dev-environment' \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --delete-all \
  --yes

# Option C: Delete stack and only delete resource groups (keep individual resources)
az stack sub delete \
  --name 'landing-zone-baseline' \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --delete-resource-groups \
  --yes

# Verify stack is gone
az stack sub show \
  --name 'landing-zone-baseline' \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB 2>&1 || echo 'Stack deleted successfully'

# After deletion with detach: verify resources still exist
az group show \
  --name 'rg-workloads-prod-eastus' \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --query 'properties.provisioningState' -o tsv
Importing Existing Resources into a Stack

Deployment Stacks can adopt resources already deployed in Azure without redeploying them. Export the existing resource group as an ARM template, decompile to Bicep, and run az stack sub create targeting the same subscription. ARM detects that the resources match existing state and performs no-op deployments — the stack simply adds them to its managed set. This adoption pattern is the primary migration path from ARM deployments, blueprint assignments, or manually created resources into stack-governed management.

# Import existing Azure resources into a new Deployment Stack
# Use when adopting stack management for resources already in Azure.
# The stack template must match the existing resource configuration exactly
# (or close enough that ARM considers the deployment idempotent).

TARGET_SUB='<landing-zone-sub-id>'

# Step 1: Export the existing resource group as an ARM template
az group export \
  --name 'rg-workloads-prod-eastus' \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --include-parameter-default-value \
  -o json > existing_workloads.json

# Step 2: Decompile to Bicep and clean up
az bicep decompile --file existing_workloads.json

# Step 3: Create the stack targeting the same resource group
# The stack will find the existing resources and add them to its managed set
# without redeploying them (idempotent ARM PUT)
az stack sub create \
  --name 'landing-zone-baseline' \
  --location eastus \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --template-file existing_workloads.bicep \
  --parameters resourceGroupName='rg-workloads-prod-eastus' location='eastus' \
  --deny-settings-mode DenyDelete \
  --deny-settings-apply-to-child-scopes \
  --action-on-unmanage detachAll

# Step 4: Verify the stack tracks the existing resources
az stack sub show \
  --name 'landing-zone-baseline' \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --query 'resources[].id' -o tsv
Common Resource Lifecycle Mistakes
Mistake
Consequence
Fix
Using deleteAll on a production stack managing shared resources
Removing a single resource from the template triggers deletion of ALL other managed resources when the stack is deleted
Use detachAll for all production stacks; reserve deleteAll only for disposable dev/sandbox environments
Removing a policy assignment from the template without knowing actionOnUnmanage
With deleteAll: policy assignment is immediately deleted; subscription is no longer enforced. With detachAll: policy remains active but unmanaged
Always run az stack sub validate before az stack sub set to preview which resources are affected by removal
Not running az stack sub validate before updates that remove resources
Unexpected resource deletion or detach — especially risky in production environments with deleteAll
Always validate first: az stack sub validate produces a what-if output showing Create/Modify/Delete effects
Deleting the stack expecting workloads to be cleaned up (when using detachAll)
Resources remain in Azure after stack deletion; RGs, VMs, storage all persist and continue billing
If cleanup is desired, use --delete-all or --delete-resource-groups explicitly; detachAll only removes the stack object
Creating a second stack in the same subscription with overlapping resources
ARM does not prevent multiple stacks from managing the same resource; duplicate tracking causes update conflicts
Design stacks to manage distinct resource sets; use resource group boundaries or naming conventions to avoid overlap
Forgetting to re-add detached resources that should remain managed
Detached resources lose deny protection; they can be deleted by users with appropriate RBAC
After detaching a resource intentionally, either re-add it to the template in the next update or apply a separate ARM lock if protection is still needed
Multi-Stack Architecture

For complex landing zones, split resources across multiple stacks based on change frequency and criticality. Network foundation (VNet, UDRs, NSGs) changes rarely and warrants DenyWriteAndDelete — changes should go through a formal change management process. Governance baseline (policy assignments, role definitions) updates monthly — DenyDelete is appropriate. Application infrastructure (VMs, App Services, databases) changes frequently and the app team needs flexibility — deny mode None with deleteAll enables full cleanup when environments are torn down. Each stack has independent deny settings and actionOnUnmanage, allowing fine-grained governance per concern.

# Multi-stack pattern: separate infrastructure concerns into independent stacks
# Each stack has its own lifecycle, deny settings, and actionOnUnmanage.

TARGET_SUB='<landing-zone-sub-id>'
BREAK_GLASS_OID='<break-glass-oid>'

# Stack 1: Networking foundation (longest-lived, strictest protection)
# Hub spoke VNet peering, UDRs, NSGs — changes are infrequent and high-impact
az stack sub create \
  --name 'networking-foundation' \
  --location eastus \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --template-file networking.bicep \
  --parameters @networking.parameters.json \
  --deny-settings-mode DenyWriteAndDelete \
  --deny-settings-apply-to-child-scopes \
  --deny-settings-excluded-principals $BREAK_GLASS_OID \
  --action-on-unmanage detachAll

# Stack 2: Governance baseline (policies, role assignments — updated monthly)
# Less strict than networking; deny protects but allows governance iteration
az stack sub create \
  --name 'governance-baseline' \
  --location eastus \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --template-file governance.bicep \
  --parameters @governance.parameters.json \
  --deny-settings-mode DenyDelete \
  --deny-settings-apply-to-child-scopes \
  --action-on-unmanage detachAll

# Stack 3: Application infrastructure (changes frequently, shorter lifecycle)
# VMs, App Services, databases — app team has excluded-principal access
az stack sub create \
  --name 'app-infrastructure' \
  --location eastus \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --template-file app_infra.bicep \
  --parameters @app_infra.parameters.json \
  --deny-settings-mode None \
  --action-on-unmanage deleteAll

# List all stacks in the subscription
az stack sub list \
  --subscription $TARGET_SUB \
  --query '[].{Name:name, Status:provisioningState, DenyMode:denySettings.mode, ActionOnUnmanage:actionOnUnmanage.resources}' \
  -o table
CI/CD Pipeline for Stack Lifecycle

The standard Deployment Stack CI/CD pattern: validate on every PR using az stack sub validate (what-if mode, no changes applied), apply on merge to main using az stack sub set (the update is idempotent). Add a safety gate in the validate step that fails the pipeline if the what-if output contains any Delete change type — this prevents accidental removal of resources under detachAll (which would silently leave them unprotected) and prevents catastrophic deletion under deleteAll. The --environment: production GitHub Actions gate requires manual approval before the deploy job runs.

# Full CI/CD pipeline pattern for Deployment Stack lifecycle management
# Each PR does what-if; merge to main applies the stack update.

# .github/workflows/stack-deploy.yml
cat << 'EOF'
name: Deployment Stack Update

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - 'stacks/**'
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - 'stacks/**'

permissions:
  id-token: write
  contents: read

jobs:
  validate:
    name: Validate Stack Changes
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      matrix:
        stack: [networking-foundation, governance-baseline, app-infrastructure]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: azure/login@v2
        with:
          client-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
          tenant-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
          subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}
      - name: Validate ${{ matrix.stack }}
        run: |
          az stack sub validate \
            --name ${{ matrix.stack }} \
            --location eastus \
            --template-file stacks/${{ matrix.stack }}/main.bicep \
            --parameters @stacks/${{ matrix.stack }}/prod.parameters.json \
            --deny-settings-mode DenyDelete \
            --action-on-unmanage detachAll 2>&1 | tee validate_output.txt
          # Fail if any 'Delete' operations are in the what-if (safety gate)
          if grep -q '"changeType": "Delete"' validate_output.txt; then
            echo 'ERROR: What-if shows resource deletions. Review before merging.'
            exit 1
          fi

  deploy:
    name: Deploy Stack
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: validate
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && github.event_name == 'push'
    environment: production
    strategy:
      matrix:
        stack: [networking-foundation, governance-baseline, app-infrastructure]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: azure/login@v2
        with:
          client-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_CLIENT_ID }}
          tenant-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_TENANT_ID }}
          subscription-id: ${{ secrets.AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID }}
      - name: Update ${{ matrix.stack }}
        run: |
          az stack sub set \
            --name ${{ matrix.stack }} \
            --location eastus \
            --template-file stacks/${{ matrix.stack }}/main.bicep \
            --parameters @stacks/${{ matrix.stack }}/prod.parameters.json \
            --deny-settings-mode DenyDelete \
            --deny-settings-apply-to-child-scopes \
            --action-on-unmanage detachAll
EOF
Summary

Deployment Stack resource lifecycle is controlled by two settings: actionOnUnmanage (detachAll or deleteAll) determines what happens when resources are removed from the template, and deny settings protect managed resources from unauthorized changes. Use detachAll for production stacks to ensure resources survive governance changes. Use deleteAll for disposable dev environments where full teardown is desired. Always run az stack sub validate before updates that remove resources. Adopt existing resources into stacks by writing a Bicep template that matches the current Azure state and running az stack sub create — no redeployment occurs.
